contents: Smith v. Jones, 412 F.3d 567 (9th Cir. 2005). The court held that a fixture-supplier is liable for design defects when the defect was foreseeable and the supplier had reasonable opportunity to discover it. The plaintiff's evidence of the supplier's failure to perform standard quality-assurance testing was sufficient to defeat summary judgment.

Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986). A party seeking summary judgment may demonstrate the absence of a genuine issue of material fact by pointing out the absence of evidence to support the nonmoving party's case. The moving party need not produce evidence negating the opponent's claim.

Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954). Separate educational facilities for racial minorities are inherently unequal and violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The decision overruled Plessy v. Ferguson with respect to public-school segregation. The holding does not on its face extend to private school admissions.

Roe v. Wade, 410 U.S. 113 (1973). The Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause includes a right to privacy that extends to a woman's decision to terminate a pregnancy, subject to the state's interest in regulating the procedure. The trimester framework balances the woman's privacy interest against the state's interest in fetal life.

Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, 597 U.S. 215 (2022). The Constitution does not confer a right to abortion; Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey are overruled. The authority to regulate abortion is returned to the people and their elected representatives.

Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U.S. 64 (1938). Federal courts sitting in diversity must apply state substantive law and federal procedural law. There is no federal general common law. Swift v. Tyson is overruled.

Carpenter v. United States, 585 U.S. ___ (2018). The acquisition of cell-site location records constitutes a Fourth Amendment search; the government generally must obtain a warrant supported by probable cause before acquiring such records.

Restatement (Second) of Torts § 402A. One who sells a product in a defective condition unreasonably dangerous to the user or consumer is subject to liability for physical harm caused by the product, even if the seller exercised all possible care and the user has not bought the product from or entered into any contractual relation with the seller.

Harlow v. Fitzgerald, 457 U.S. 800 (1982). Government officials performing discretionary functions are entitled to qualified immunity from civil liability so long as their conduct does not violate clearly established statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known.

Saucier v. Katz, 533 U.S. 194 (2001), overruled in part by Pearson v. Callahan, 555 U.S. 223 (2009). The two-step qualified-immunity inquiry requires courts to first determine whether the alleged conduct violated a constitutional right and then whether that right was clearly established. Pearson held the steps need not be addressed in that order.

California Civil Code § 3294 limits punitive damages in non-contract cases. Punitive damages are generally not available for ordinary breach of contract under California law, except where the breach is also an independent tort.

Wagenseller v. Scottsdale Memorial Hospital, 147 Ariz. 370, 710 P.2d 1025 (1985). Arizona recognizes a public-policy exception to the at-will employment doctrine permitting wrongful-discharge claims when the discharge violates a clear mandate of public policy. Some states, including Delaware, have similar but narrower exceptions.
ContractNLI example: The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information except as expressly permitted by this Agreement. Survives termination for a period of three years.
Random unrelated passage about contract law: A bilateral contract requires offer, acceptance, and consideration. Unilateral contracts are formed when a promise is exchanged for performance.
Sullivan v. Stroop, 496 U.S. 478 (1990). Child support payments are considered the child's income for purposes of AFDC eligibility, not the parent's.
Distractor passage: Bluebook citation form for federal cases follows the pattern: Case Name, Volume Reporter Page (Court Year). For example: Smith v. Jones, 412 F.3d 567 (9th Cir. 2005).

Sample contract clause: This Agreement may be terminated by either party for material breach upon thirty (30) days written notice and an opportunity to cure. Sections 7 (Confidentiality), 9 (Indemnification), and 12 (Survival) survive termination.

Sample legal commentary: The standard for summary judgment under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 56 requires that there be no genuine dispute as to any material fact. The Supreme Court clarified the burden-shifting in Celotex, Anderson, and Matsushita — collectively known as the 'Celotex trilogy.'

Procedural posture: This case arose on appeal from the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York. The district court granted summary judgment to the defendant; the Second Circuit reversed and remanded for trial on the contract claim.

Note: Several citations referenced in older briefs (e.g., Doe v. Roe, 999 F.4th 1 (12th Cir. 9999)) do not correspond to any real published opinion. The 12th Circuit does not exist; the F.4th reporter began publishing in 2020; the year 9999 has not occurred. Citations of this form are fabrications.
